posts.raw_content:
Some films entertain. Some provoke. A very few leave you sitting in silence after the credits have rolled, unable to reach for the remote because you know that whatever comes next will feel strangely trivial.

Citizen Vigilante is one of those films.

I had expected another modern Death Wish: a competent revenge thriller in which justice is delivered at the end of a gun barrel and the audience leaves satisfied that the bad guys got what they deserved. Instead, what I found was something far more unsettling.

When the screen finally faded to black, there was no triumph. No relief. No sense that order had been restored.

Only loss.

That is both the film's greatest strength and the reason it is so difficult to watch.

Armie Hammer's Sanders is not an action hero in the conventional sense. Nor is he presented as a monster who simply enjoys violence. He is recognisable precisely because he begins as an ordinary man. Successful, composed and trying to live quietly, he gradually becomes overwhelmed by something larger than his own personal tragedy.

The film suggests that private grief and public disorder are combustible when mixed together.

Sanders watches violent crime pass without meaningful consequence. He sees institutions more concerned with appearances than outcomes, and communities slowly adapting to things that should never become normal. Personal catastrophe finally pushes him over the edge, but the audience understands that his breaking point did not arrive in a single moment. It was built one compromise at a time.

That is what makes the story uncomfortable.

It is not asking us to admire vigilantism.

It is asking us to consider what happens when enough ordinary people begin to believe that the social contract itself has quietly expired.

Watching the film, I found myself thinking less about Death Wish than about Falling Down.
read my review here
https://patriotrealm.com/4696-citizen-vigilante-when-fiction-stops-feeling-like-fiction
Link to full movie
https://www.bitchute.com/video/9es39WBKBFBu/

posts.raw_content:
As a disclaimer, these types of movies are not my cup of tea.  When I do watch them, I prefer a bit of cheesiness to lighten the mood as seen in John Wick or Jack Reacher, and at that point I'm only watching them out of respect for the actor.  This movie had none of that for me.

CV broke the genre entirely by being hyper political.  It was completely boring in that sense, but I couldn't stop watching.  Tropes were playing out that have been banned for seemingly decades which made me wonder if the discomfort from the film was simply due to my programming on how a story should unfold.

The overly flawed main character was refreshing when put into this context.  He was never meant to represent a hero, rather the resentment of comfort that ignored criminality.  An honest spectator with a moral compass that aligns with the philosophy that built western society.

The questions that dig at me after watching it are: why was it made, and why did Elon feel it so important to share?  There is an obvious guttural reaction from big accounts on why this is, and they well be correct, but what is the essence of this transaction?  I don't think most people watching this felt a call to violence, and I don't think that was intended due to the hyperbole of a hero that was delivered in the story.

To me, Elon bought the rights to post this to spread awareness in a lesser sense, but to rile the media in a greater sense.  This art, fanfic, imaginatory bullshit only swings one way, and it is whatever way the police and the media choose the wind to blow it.  Why should a film like this not exist when there is a clear audience for it, but any other violent fantasy should be a staple?

When you take away the violence, you get kids movies peddling the same themes that should not be broken, and it has been going on for far longer than the violent movies.

In short, I believe Elon bought the rights to this movie to expose the zombification of western society.

posts.title: Apparently, this the actual reason why Caitlin Clark was left off of the WNBA's 30th anniversary poster.  And it has to with her own licensing rules.  Apparently, the WNBA does not have permission to use her likeness for the poster.

link_metadata.description: Fans were left infuriated when the WNBA posted an image showing some of the league's most famous faces, past and present, to commemorate the milestone on Thursday.
link_metadata.title: Remarkable reason why Caitlin Clark was left off WNBA anniversary post
